feedback.text: I am writing as a concerned consumer and lifelong gamer to support the Stop Killing Games initiative. I believe its time the EU took a serious look at how video gamesespecially those we buy digitallyare treated in terms of ownership and long-term access. When I buy a game, whether physically or online, I expect to be able to play it in the future. Increasingly, though, games Ive paid for are simply being shut downsometimes without proper notice, sometimes even if theyre single-player experiences that just happen to rely on online verification. This feels fundamentally wrong. In any other mediabooks, movies, musicI wouldnt accept this. Why should games be different? Over 1.2 million people across Europe have now signed the "Stop Killing Games" petition. That says a lot. It reflects the growing frustration people feel when their digital libraries are erased, not because of anything they did, but because a company decided to shut off access. Its not just about entertainment. Games are cultural works. We wouldnt tolerate burning down libraries or removing movies from public access, yet thats essentially whats happening with video games. One study found that 87% of classic U.S. games released before 2010 are no longer preserved or available in any form. Once these games disappear, theyre gone for good. This problem is only getting worse. In 2024, about 75% of all game sales in Europe were digital. That means more and more of us are buying products that can vanish overnight. Were being sold licenses, not ownershipoften without clarity or recourse. I believe the EU should take action by: Requiring publishers to provide offline support or allow players to run private servers when a game is discontinued Ensuring consumers get proper notice when a game is being shut down, and ideally, a way to keep playing it Making it clear that digital purchases should remain accessible after official support endsespecially when people have spent money on additional content I'm not asking for endless support from developers. Im asking for fairness. If a game I paid for is going offline, I should still be able to play it. If companies cant guarantee that, then we need laws that do. Thank you for considering this. I really hope the EU can lead the way on protecting digital ownership and game preservation before more of our cultureand our purchasesare lost.
